trip.name: Patagonia Explorer — El Chaltén, El Calafate & Torres del Paine
trip.youtube_overview: Patagonia El Chaltén El Calafate Torres del Paine hiking guide
trip.description: A nine-day journey through the heart of Patagonia, spanning Argentine and Chilean highlights. The trip begins in El Chaltén — Argentina's trekking capital — with hikes to Laguna Torre beneath the spires of Cerro Torre, and panoramic viewpoints above the Río de las Vueltas valley. It then moves to El Calafate for a full day at the Perito Moreno Glacier, one of the only advancing glaciers on Earth, combining walkway viewing, boat safari, and sunset kayaking. The final four nights are spent at Hotel Las Torres inside Torres del Paine National Park, with guided day hikes to the iconic Base of the Towers, the dramatic French Valley, and a van-supported grand tour of the park's lakes, waterfalls, and Grey Glacier. Expect big winds, bigger mountains, and some of the most dramatic scenery on the planet.

days.label: El Calafate to El Chaltén
days.description:
A scenic three-hour drive north from El Calafate through the Patagonian steppe to El Chaltén, the small mountain village at the foot of the Fitz Roy massif. The road crosses flat, wind-swept grassland before the landscape transforms as you approach Los Glaciares National Park. About halfway, the route passes La Leona Roadhouse — a historic parador from the 1890s — and a viewpoint over Lago Viedma where the distant Viedma Glacier comes into view.

As the bus rounds the final bends into El Chaltén, the jagged silhouettes of Cerro Torre and Monte Fitz Roy appear above the valley. Use the afternoon to explore the village, stock up on supplies for tomorrow's trek, and check weather forecasts at the park ranger station near the entrance to town. El Chaltén has a handful of gear shops, bakeries, and restaurants — small-town Patagonia at its best.
days.resupply_notes: Several small supermarkets in El Chaltén (La Anónima, Poirot). Stock up on trail snacks, water, and sunscreen. ATMs are unreliable — bring cash from El Calafate.

days.label: Laguna Torre Trek
days.description:
The Laguna Torre trail is one of El Chaltén's two iconic hikes, leading 9 km into the Fitz Roy river valley to a glacial lake at the foot of Cerro Torre — one of the most technically difficult peaks in the world. On a clear day, the needle-like granite tower rises above the turquoise lake with Glacier Grande spilling into it, a sight that ranks among Patagonia's finest.

The trailhead starts at the western edge of El Chaltén. Most of the elevation gain happens in the first 2 km as you climb through lenga forest to the first viewpoint above the Fitz Roy River canyon, where Margarita Waterfall is visible across the gorge. After this initial climb, the trail levels off and follows the river valley for several kilometres through open woodland and meadows, passing a midway viewpoint with panoramic views of the Adela Range and Cerro Torre. The final approach to the lagoon crosses rocky terrain before opening onto the lake shore, where icebergs calved from the glacier float in milky-blue water.

For the best views, continue past the main lakeside viewpoint to Mirador Maestri on the ridge above — an extra 20-minute scramble that gives an in-your-face perspective of Glacier Grande. Return is via the same route. Your guide will accompany you throughout the day.
days.resupply_notes: Carry your own lunch and at least 1.5L of water. Streams along the trail are drinkable. Shops in El Chaltén for resupply on return.
water_sources: Streams throughout the trail — water is safe to drink directly
Río Fitz Roy (early in the trail)
Campamento De Agostini area (near the lagoon)
escape_routes: Return the same way to El Chaltén — no alternative exit routes
Campamento De Agostini at km 8 is the last sheltered spot before the lagoon
warnings: Wind can be extreme at the lagoon — gusts over 80 km/h are common. Secure loose items.
The final rocky section near the lagoon is uneven — watch your footing
Start early to maximise chances of clear views; Cerro Torre clouds over frequently by midday
Two dry composting toilets along the trail — bring your own toilet paper

days.label: Cóndores & Águilas Viewpoints + Transfer to El Calafate
days.description:
A gentle half-day hike to two of El Chaltén's finest viewpoints before the afternoon transfer back to El Calafate. Mirador de los Cóndores sits on a bluff directly above town, offering a sweeping panorama of Monte Fitz Roy, the Cerro Torre range, and the Río de las Vueltas valley far below — all framed by the huge Patagonian sky. As the name suggests, Andean condors frequently ride the thermals here, especially from late morning to mid-afternoon.

The trail starts at the park ranger station on Ruta 41 at the entrance to El Chaltén. A steady 40-minute climb on a clear path brings you to the Cóndores viewpoint. From there, a mostly flat 1-km extension leads to Mirador de las Águilas, which swaps the mountain view for a completely different perspective — the vast expanse of Lago Viedma and the Patagonian steppe stretching to the horizon. The loop trail returns you to the ranger station.

After the hike, return to town to collect your luggage and board the shared transfer back to El Calafate (approximately 3 hours). You'll arrive in the evening with time to settle into your hotel and prepare for tomorrow's glacier day.
days.resupply_notes: El Calafate has supermarkets (La Anónima), ATMs, and outdoor gear shops along Avenida Libertador. Stock up for the glacier day.
water_sources: Carry water from El Chaltén — no water sources on the short trail
escape_routes: The trail is short and returns directly to El Chaltén — no bailout needed
warnings: Exposed hilltop — wind can be fierce. Bring a windproof layer.
The final section to Cóndores viewpoint can be slippery on loose rock

days.label: Perito Moreno Glacier — Walkways, Boat Safari & Kayaking
days.description:
A packed day experiencing one of the world's great natural wonders from three different perspectives. The Perito Moreno Glacier is 30 km long, 5 km wide at its face, and rises 60-70 metres above the waterline — and unlike most glaciers, it is still advancing at roughly 2 metres per day. The constant cracking, groaning, and thunderous calving of house-sized ice blocks into Lago Argentino is a spectacle you feel as much as see.

The morning begins with the 80 km drive from El Calafate to the glacier inside Los Glaciares National Park. The walkway network on the Magallanes Peninsula consists of 4.7 km of elevated metal boardwalks divided into colour-coded circuits at different heights and angles, each offering a distinct perspective of the north and south faces. Budget at least 90 minutes to walk the main circuits. Mid-morning, the Nautical Safari boat takes you across the Canal de los Témpanos to within 300 metres of the glacier's towering north wall — a completely different scale from water level. The afternoon brings sunset kayaking on Lago Argentino's Brazo Rico arm, paddling among small icebergs with the glacier as your backdrop.

Expect to be on your feet for much of the day between the walkways, boat boarding, and kayaking put-in, but none of it is physically demanding. Bring layers — the glacier generates its own cold microclimate.
days.resupply_notes: A restaurant, snack bar, and gift shop are at the walkway parking area. Bring a packed lunch or buy food on-site.
water_sources: Potable water in the restrooms at the walkway visitor area
Cafe and restaurant at the walkway parking area
escape_routes: The walkways are fully accessible and return to the parking area — no exposure
Boat and kayak excursions have safety briefings and life jackets provided
warnings: Do not cross safety barriers on the walkways — falling ice can send waves and debris at the shore
Glacier generates cold winds — dress in warm layers and bring a windproof jacket even on sunny days
Strong sun combined with cold wind is deceptive — wear sunscreen and sunglasses
National park entrance fee required — purchase online in advance to avoid queues

days.label: El Calafate to Hotel Las Torres (Torres del Paine)
days.description:
A full-day transfer from Argentina to Chile, crossing the international border at Cancha Carrera (Argentina) and Cerro Castillo (Chile) before arriving at Hotel Las Torres in the heart of Torres del Paine National Park. The Las Torres shuttle departs El Calafate early morning and the journey takes approximately 5-6 hours including border formalities. The route crosses the Patagonian steppe with wide-open views, and the stop in Cerro Castillo offers a chance to stretch your legs and take in the landscape.

Upon arrival at Hotel Las Torres — positioned at the trailhead for the Base Torres trek — you'll have the afternoon free to settle in, explore the hotel's self-guided Conservation Trail (about 30 minutes), and attend the evening excursion briefing where guides present the next day's options. The hotel sits on a working estancia with 200 horses, and you may spot guanacos grazing on the surrounding plains. Dinner is included and served in the hotel restaurant.
days.resupply_notes: No shops inside Torres del Paine. The hotel provides all meals (packed lunch for excursions). Buy any last-minute items in El Calafate before departure.
warnings: Bring your passport — this is an international border crossing between Argentina and Chile
Border processing can take 30-60 minutes depending on queues
Chile has strict biosecurity rules — no fresh fruit, vegetables, meat, or dairy products can be brought across the border

days.label: Base of the Towers Trek
days.description:
The crown jewel of Torres del Paine — an 18 km round-trip trek from Hotel Las Torres to the Mirador Base de las Torres, the iconic viewpoint beneath the park's three granite towers. These spires soar to nearly 2,850 metres and the turquoise glacial lake at their base is one of the most photographed sights in South America. This is a demanding but deeply rewarding day.

The trail starts directly from the hotel, crossing a suspension bridge over the Ascencio River before climbing steeply through lenga forest for about 2 km. The gradient eases as you reach the Windy Pass — appropriately named — where the valley opens up with mountain views on both sides. From here the trail follows the Ascencio River valley through forest to Refugio Chileno (5.5 km from the hotel), which has basic facilities and a small cafe. Beyond Chileno, the trail continues through forest to Campamento Torres, where you leave the trees behind.

The final kilometre is the hardest: a 400-metre scramble up a steep glacial moraine of loose boulders and scree. Footing is uncertain and the wind can be ferocious. But when you crest the moraine and see the three towers reflected in the emerald lake below, every step is justified. Allow 45 minutes to an hour for this final push, and plan to spend time at the top for lunch and photos before retracing your steps to the hotel.
days.resupply_notes: Hotel provides a packed lunch box for excursions (sandwich, fruit, nuts, cereal bars). Fill your water bottles at the hotel before departure.
water_sources: Streams throughout the Ascencio Valley — drinkable without treatment
escape_routes: Turn back at Refugio Chileno (5.5 km) if weather deteriorates or fatigue sets in — still a beautiful forest walk
Campamento Torres (8 km) is the last sheltered spot before the exposed moraine
warnings: The final moraine scramble is steep and exposed — loose boulders require careful footing and strong ankles
Wind at the Windy Pass and at the viewpoint can exceed 80 km/h — brace yourself and secure all gear
Start early — trail officially closes at Guardería Torres at 3:45 PM and at the viewpoint by 4:30 PM
Weather changes rapidly. Carry waterproof layers even if the morning is clear.
Suggested minimum age is 12 years for this trek

days.label: French Valley Hike
days.description:
The longest and most dramatic day of the trip takes you deep into the French Valley — the central arm of Torres del Paine's famous W Trek. The excursion begins with a drive to Pudeto, where you board a catamaran across the stunning turquoise Lake Pehoé to Paine Grande. From there, the trail follows the north shore of Lake Skottsberg before turning into the French Valley itself.

The valley is a natural amphitheatre of staggering proportions: hanging glaciers cling to the walls of Paine Grande (the park's highest peak at 3,050m), while the dark horns of Los Cuernos frame the view to the east. Cascading waterfalls feed into the valley from melting ice above. The trail climbs steeply through lenga forest before emerging above the treeline at the French Valley lookout, where the panorama includes Lakes Nordenskjöld, Pehoé, and Toro spread out below, with Hoja, Máscara, Espada, Fortaleza, and Aleta de Tiburón peaks surrounding you.

This is a full 12-hour day including the boat crossings. The terrain is demanding, with sustained climbing in the upper valley. Your legs will feel yesterday's Base Torres effort, so pace yourself carefully. The reward is one of the most spectacular mountain vistas in all of Patagonia.
days.resupply_notes: Hotel provides packed lunch. Carry at least 2L of water — there are refill opportunities from streams in the valley.
water_sources: Streams in the French Valley — drinkable without treatment
Water available at Refugio Paine Grande (start/end of hike)
escape_routes: Turn back at Campamento Italiano (approx. km 7) — the valley entrance — if conditions worsen
Refugio Paine Grande (near the catamaran dock) is the nearest full-service shelter
warnings: Very long day — 12 hours total including transport. Start early and maintain a steady pace.
Cumulative fatigue from Day 6 will be noticeable — stretch and hydrate well the evening before
Upper French Valley is exposed to high winds and can receive snow even in November
Catamaran schedules are fixed — do not miss the return boat from Paine Grande
Trail above the treeline has some scrambling sections over rock

days.label: Full Paine Grand Tour
days.description:
After two strenuous trekking days, today offers a gentler pace while still covering the park's greatest hits. The Full Paine tour travels by van to a series of viewpoints and short hikes across the park, giving you perspectives that the trekking routes don't reach. It's a sampler of Torres del Paine's extraordinary diversity — from the desert-like steppe to turquoise lakes, roaring waterfalls, and the massive Grey Glacier.

The route includes stops at Puente Negro (Black Bridge) for a view into the Ascencio Valley, Mirador Nordenskjöld with its sweeping panorama of the lake and Los Cuernos, and Mirador Sarmiento overlooking the prehistoric-blue waters of Lake Sarmiento with its thrombolite rock formations. At Lake Pehoé, you'll walk to the thundering Salto Grande waterfall, which channels water between Lakes Nordenskjöld and Pehoé. The final highlight is a choice between a hike along the southern shore of Lake Grey — where icebergs from Grey Glacier float past in otherworldly blues — or a catamaran ride across the lake to the glacier's face.

With about 10 km of walking spread across several short hikes and plenty of van time between stops, this is a welcome recovery day that still delivers world-class scenery.
days.resupply_notes: Hotel provides packed lunch. The Grey Glacier area has a small cafe/shop near the catamaran dock.
water_sources: Water available at each van stop — carry a bottle
escape_routes: Van-based tour — you are never far from the vehicle
The guide can adjust the itinerary if weather deteriorates
warnings: Lake Grey can be very windy — the catamaran may be cancelled in extreme conditions
Wear layers — conditions vary significantly between sheltered valleys and exposed lakeshores
Guanaco herds frequently cross the road — keep a respectful distance

days.label: Hotel Las Torres to Puerto Natales
days.description:
The final morning at Hotel Las Torres. After breakfast, board the shared shuttle for the 2-hour drive south to Puerto Natales, the gateway town on the shore of Última Esperanza Sound. The road passes through open steppe with views back towards the Paine massif gradually receding behind you — a last look at the granite towers and ice-capped peaks that defined the past four days.

Puerto Natales is a colourful, walkable town with a strong frontier feel. It serves as the hub for Torres del Paine tourism and has a good selection of restaurants, cafes, and shops. Depending on your onward travel plans, you can catch a flight from Puerto Natales airport, take a bus to Punta Arenas (3 hours), or continue exploring the Última Esperanza Province.
days.resupply_notes: Puerto Natales has supermarkets, ATMs, gear shops, and pharmacies along its main streets. Good place to buy souvenirs and restock.

waypoints.name: Hotel Las Torres (departure)
waypoints.note: Farewell. Las Torres shuttle departs after breakfast.

waypoints.name: Puerto Natales (arrival)

weather.description: Late November marks the beginning of the Patagonian summer. Expect long days with up to 16 hours of daylight, but conditions are highly variable. Temperatures range from near-freezing at dawn to a pleasant 15°C in the afternoon sun. The defining feature of Patagonian weather is the wind — sustained winds of 30-50 km/h are normal, with gusts exceeding 80 km/h at exposed viewpoints and passes. Rain and even snow can arrive without warning, often followed by sunshine within the hour. The key to comfort is layering: you may need sunscreen and a down jacket in the same morning. El Chaltén tends to be slightly warmer and less windy than Torres del Paine, which sits in a more exposed position.
alerts: Wind is the biggest challenge — gusts can knock you off balance on exposed ridges and passes
UV radiation is high due to thin ozone layer in southern latitudes — wear SPF 50+ sunscreen and quality sunglasses
Weather changes rapidly — always carry waterproof layers, even if the morning is clear
Snow is possible above 600m, even in November

gear_checklist.item: Moisture-wicking base layer top and bottom
Waterproof and windproof shell jacket (Gore-Tex or similar)
Trekking poles (highly recommended for the moraine on Base Torres)
Waterproof dry bag or pack liner
Reusable water bottle or hydration bladder (1.5-2L)
Offline maps downloaded (AllTrails, Maps.me, or Wikiloc)
Basic first aid kit (blister plasters, painkillers, anti-chafing)
Toilet paper and waste bags (for trail toilets)
Passport and copies of travel insurance
Camera with spare battery (cold drains batteries fast)
Trail snacks (energy bars, nuts, dried fruit, chocolate)

preparation.overview: This trip combines moderate-to-strenuous day hikes with substantial driving and boat transfers. The two hardest days — Base Torres (Day 6) and French Valley (Day 7) — come back-to-back in the second half of the trip, so arriving with a solid fitness base is important. The physical demands are not extreme for experienced hikers, but the Patagonian wind, unpredictable weather, and cumulative fatigue over nine days mean that preparation pays off significantly in comfort and enjoyment.

training_schedule.title: Build Base Endurance
training_schedule.description: Focus on building aerobic endurance and getting your legs accustomed to sustained walking. Include hills wherever possible — the Base Torres trek involves 850m of elevation gain, with a steep final moraine section.
activities: Walk 10-15 km twice per week, including at least one hilly route
One longer walk of 15-20 km on weekends
Include stair climbing or incline treadmill sessions 2-3 times per week
Begin wearing your hiking boots on all training walks to break them in

training_schedule.title: Build Trek-Specific Fitness
training_schedule.description: Increase intensity and simulate the demands of back-to-back hiking days. The French Valley (Day 7) follows immediately after Base Torres (Day 6), so your body needs to handle consecutive strenuous days.
activities: Two back-to-back long walks of 15-20 km over a weekend
Add a weighted daypack (8-10 kg) to simulate carrying water, lunch, and layers
Include one hike with 600-800m elevation gain per week if terrain is available
Practise walking on rocky/uneven terrain to prepare for the moraine

training_schedule.title: Sharpen and Taper
training_schedule.description: Maintain fitness but reduce volume. Focus on feeling fresh and confident for departure day.
activities: One moderate hike of 10-12 km mid-week
Light walks of 5-8 km on other days
Stretch and foam roll daily — focus on calves, quads, and hip flexors
Finalise gear and test all equipment on a short training walk

training_schedule.title: Rest and Prepare
training_schedule.description: Prioritise rest, hydration, and logistics. Your fitness is locked in — now focus on arriving well-rested and organised.
activities: Hydrate well and focus on nutrition
Pack your bag and check all gear against the checklist
Download offline maps and confirm all bookings

preparation.physical_demands: The trip includes two strenuous hiking days back-to-back (18 km with 850m gain, followed by 20 km with 750m gain), with the added challenge of Patagonian wind that can turn a moderate trail into an exhausting slog. You need to be comfortable walking 18-20 km on rough terrain in a single day, and able to repeat it the following day. The moraine scramble on Base Torres requires reasonable agility on loose boulders. Transfer days provide welcome rest, but the overall pace across nine days is sustained.
recommended_training: Build to comfortably walking 18-20 km in a single day with 800m elevation gain
Practise two consecutive long walking days to prepare for Days 6 and 7
Train with a weighted daypack (8-10 kg) to simulate carrying water, food, and layers
Include rocky or uneven terrain in your training to prepare for the Base Torres moraine
Strengthen your ankles — the moraine and Patagonian trails have plenty of loose rock
Break in your hiking boots completely before the trip — blisters on Day 2 will ruin everything
what_to_expect: Day 1: Transfer day — no physical demands. Use the afternoon to walk around El Chaltén and acclimatise to the Patagonian wind.

Day 2: Laguna Torre (18 km, 490m gain). A solid full-day hike but manageable for anyone with reasonable fitness. The initial climb is the hardest part; the valley walk is relatively flat. Expect tired legs by evening.
Day 3: Cóndores & Águilas (5 km, 215m gain) + transfer. A gentle morning hike — perfect for recovery. Short and steep but over quickly. Legs may feel the previous day on the climb.
Day 4: Perito Moreno Glacier day. Mostly walking on flat metal walkways — no real physical demand. Kayaking requires basic upper body effort. A good recovery day for your legs.
Day 5: Transfer day — no physical demands. Long drive but restful. Use the evening to stretch and prepare mentally for the big days ahead.

Day 6: Base Torres (18 km, 850m gain). The most demanding day. The first 8 km are steady but manageable. The final moraine scramble is a 400m climb over loose boulders that will test your legs, lungs, and patience. Expect deep fatigue in the evening.

Day 7: French Valley (20 km, 750m gain, 12 hours). Back-to-back with Base Torres — this is the trip's toughest test. Your legs will be heavy from Day 6. The distance is long, the climb is sustained, and the boat schedule means you cannot take unlimited time. Pace yourself and fuel consistently.
Day 8: Full Paine Tour (10 km, 300m gain). A welcome relief. Short hikes from the van with rest between stops. Your legs will be grateful for the reduced effort.
Day 9: Transfer day — no physical demands. Rest, reflect, and enjoy the final Patagonian views from the bus.

general_info.visa_info: Argentina: Citizens of the EU, UK, USA, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand do not require a visa for stays up to 90 days. Chile: Same visa-free access for the above nationalities for stays up to 90 days. Check current requirements for your nationality before travel, as rules can change. You will pass through immigration at the Cancha Carrera (Argentina) and Cerro Castillo (Chile) border crossing on Day 5.
general_info.health_insurance: Comprehensive travel insurance with medical evacuation cover is essential. The trekking areas are remote and helicopter evacuation to the nearest hospital (El Calafate or Punta Arenas) can be extremely expensive without insurance. Ensure your policy covers hiking up to 3,000m altitude and adventure activities including kayaking. Keep a printed copy of your policy and emergency number with you.

emergency_contacts.notes: Police, fire, and ambulance in Argentina
Torres del Paine park office for trail emergencies
Hospital Municipal de El Calafate — nearest hospital during Argentina portion
Hospital Augusto Essmann — nearest hospital during Chile portion

transport.getting_there: Fly into El Calafate Airport (FTE) from Buenos Aires (approx. 3.5 hours, Aerolíneas Argentinas or LATAM). The airport is 22 km from El Calafate town centre — taxis and airport shuttles are available. El Calafate is the starting point for the trip.
transport.getting_back: The trip ends in Puerto Natales, Chile. From there: fly from Puerto Natales Airport (PNT) to Santiago (LATAM, limited flights), take a bus to Punta Arenas (3 hours, multiple daily services by Bus-Sur) and fly from Punta Arenas Airport (PUQ) to Santiago, or arrange a private transfer back to El Calafate (5-6 hours including border crossing) to fly out from there.
transport.local_transport: Day 1 and Day 3: Shared transfer El Calafate–El Chaltén (Caltur, Chaltén Travel — approx. 3 hours, multiple daily services). Day 5: Las Torres hotel shuttle El Calafate–Hotel Las Torres (5-6 hours including border). Day 9: Las Torres shuttle to Puerto Natales (approx. 2 hours). All excursions include transport from accommodations.

general_info.currency: Argentina uses the Argentine Peso (ARS). Chile uses the Chilean Peso (CLP). US Dollars are widely accepted at hotels and tour agencies in both countries, often at favourable rates. ATMs in El Calafate are available but unreliable — bring backup USD cash. Card payments (Visa/Mastercard) are accepted at hotels, restaurants, and shops in El Calafate and Puerto Natales. Inside Torres del Paine National Park, card is accepted at Hotel Las Torres but cash is useful for small purchases. National park entrance fees for both Los Glaciares (Argentina) and Torres del Paine (Chile) are best purchased online in advance.
general_info.language: Spanish is the primary language in both Argentina and Chile. English is spoken at hotels and by tour guides throughout the trip. Useful phrases: '¿Cuánto cuesta?' (How much?), 'La cuenta, por favor' (The bill, please), '¿Dónde está...?' (Where is...?), 'No hablo español' (I don't speak Spanish).
general_info.mobile_coverage: El Chaltén: Limited 4G coverage in town (Movistar and Claro work best). No signal on the trails. El Calafate: Good 4G coverage in town. Patchy signal on the road to Perito Moreno — none at the glacier. Torres del Paine: Very limited to no mobile coverage inside the park. Hotel Las Torres has Wi-Fi for guests (can be slow). Download all maps and information before entering the park. Consider an Argentine SIM card (Movistar or Claro) and a Chilean SIM card (Entel or Movistar) for the respective countries, or use an international eSIM provider (Airalo, Holafly).
useful_apps: AllTrails — offline trail maps for all hikes
Maps.me — excellent offline maps for Patagonia
Windy.com — real-time wind and weather forecasting (essential for Patagonia)
XE Currency — live exchange rates for ARS and CLP
general_info.additional_notes: Los Glaciares National Park (Argentina) now charges an entrance fee for El Chaltén trekking — purchase online at the national parks website before arrival. Torres del Paine (Chile) requires advance ticket purchase at pasesparques.cl — tickets are valid for 3 consecutive days. Chile's strict biosecurity rules mean no fresh food (fruit, meat, dairy) can be brought across the border on Day 5 — consume or dispose of everything before the crossing. Tipping in Argentina and Chile is customary: 10% in restaurants, and a tip for hiking guides is appreciated for good service.
